items.title: Global liquidity re-accelerates to $191.8T — Fed + weak dollar boosting collateral while PBoC tightens
items.idea: Global liquidity climbed to US$191.8 trillion last week, driven by Fed support, a weaker dollar, and sharply lower bond volatility boosting collateral values. Short-term momentum has re-accelerated. However, PBoC tightening and ongoing QT elsewhere remain a structural drag. Risk exposure is stretched in Asian Emerging Markets and Japan, while other Developed Markets are broadly neutral.
items.angle: The split between developed and Asian EM risk exposures is the key positioning signal. If global liquidity re-accelerates into a CPI surprise environment, the usual risk-on playbook gets complicated — stretched EM positioning could reverse fast.
items.tweetA: Global liquidity just re-hit $191.8T. Fed support + weaker dollar + bond vol collapse = collateral surge. But two problems: PBoC is tightening, and risk exposure in Asian EM and Japan is already stretched. The fuel is there. The conditions for a reversal are also there.
items.tweetB: Western liquidity expands. Asia tightens. $191.8T of global liquidity is masking a positioning problem in EM. The places that are crowded are exactly the places that sell first when vol spikes.

items.title: April CPI: 3.8% YoY (highest since May 2023), core +0.4% MoM hottest since Jan 2025, real wages negative YoY
items.idea: US April CPI headline: +3.8% YoY, highest since May 2023. Energy (+3.8%) contributed 40% of the monthly move, but it is not only energy — groceries posted the largest monthly jump in 4 years, and airfares, hotel prices, and electronics also surged. Core CPI: +0.4% MoM (hottest since January 2025), +2.8% YoY. Core Goods essentially flat (+0.03%), suggesting limited tariff pressure so far. Core Services accelerated to +0.5% MoM (most since May 2024). Supercore re-accelerated to +0.45% MoM, 3rd highest since January 2025. Real average hourly earnings turned negative YoY (-0.21%) for the first time since May 2023 — worst two-month stretch since June 2022. Fed funds futures now price 58% chance of a rate hike by next April. Caveat: a significant portion of April's acceleration reflects a one-time statistical unwinding of a prior rent/shelter distortion since October.
items.angle: Real wages negative YoY + 58% rate hike pricing = stagflation lite. The shelter catch-up distortion adds noise, but supercore and groceries are pointing in the wrong direction for rate cuts regardless.
items.tweetA: April CPI: 3.8% YoY. But the real story is buried: real wages just turned negative YoY for the first time since 2023. You can handle high inflation OR stagnant wages. Both at once is different. Fed funds futures now price a 58% chance of a HIKE by next April.
items.tweetB: Energy is 40% of April CPI. OK, war premium. But core services re-accelerated to +0.5% MoM — most since May 2024 — and supercore hit its 3rd-highest read since January 2025. Groceries: largest monthly jump in 4 years. This is not just an oil story.

charts.desc: Multiple CPI decomposition charts: shelter catch-up, supercore trend, real wages. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Iran economy collapsing under war: rial at 1.87M/$, 1M+ jobs destroyed, IRGC losing $170M/day, drug prices +400%
items.idea: Iran entered the war carrying its worst economic crisis in the history of the Islamic Republic. The rial has collapsed from 1.45M per dollar in January to 1.87M now — roughly 29% further devaluation in months. The war directly destroyed over 1 million jobs; indirect losses add 2 million more. Mobarakeh Steel took heavy hits — 27,000 workers now earn ~$114/month down from ~$568. Steel and petrochemicals function as a single interconnected system; US strikes hit both load-bearing nodes. Petrochemical sector generates ~$13B annually — Iran's 2nd-largest FX earner after crude. Drug prices surged 400%, pharmacies report nationwide shortages. IRGC salary delays caused personnel to skip mobilization events in major cities.
items.angle: The IRGC salary delays causing troops to skip mobilization is the most consequential data point. A patronage regime that cannot pay its enforcers is in a different kind of trouble. Oil above $100 today while the supplier is in structural collapse — that tension will resolve one way or another.
items.tweetA: Iran's rial: 1.87M per dollar. That's a 29% devaluation since January — during an active war. Drug prices +400%. IRGC troops skipping mobilization because paychecks stopped. A regime held together by patronage, not just ideology, has just watched the patronage dry up.
items.tweetB: In Iran right now: 1M+ direct job losses, 2M+ indirect. Mobarakeh Steel workers earning $114/month — down from $568. Drug shortages nationwide. A central bank publicly warning citizens not to buy dollars. This is not a country that can sustain a multi-year conflict.

items.title: Operation Economic Fury: Chinese satellite firms sanctioned for supplying IRGC military intelligence; $4.8B total oil revenue destroyed
items.idea: US Treasury designated Hengli Petrochemical (China's 2nd-largest teapot refinery) and ~40 shadow fleet shipping firms. Chinese satellite companies Meentropy Technology, The Earth Eye, and Chang Guang Satellite Technology were sanctioned for supplying Iran with geospatial intelligence to monitor US and allied military movements — placing Chinese complicity on public legal record. Beijing issued a formal prohibition order directing citizens not to comply. The naval blockade has cut Kharg Island to near storage capacity; Pentagon estimates $4.8B in lost oil revenue to date. The IRGC faces a structural dilemma: deal with Trump = surrender patronage network; continue war = institutional collapse under compounding sanctions.
items.angle: The Chinese satellite designations create a documented legal predicate for broader action against Chinese commercial remote sensing firms. The IRGC dilemma is genuinely structural: not a political problem that can be negotiated, but an economic architecture that cannot survive the current environment.
items.tweetA: The US just sanctioned Chinese satellite companies for supplying Iran with geospatial intelligence to track US military movements. Beijing's response: a formal prohibition order telling Chinese firms not to comply. When a government has to publicly tell its companies not to help a terrorist-designated state, the discreet phase is over.
items.tweetB: Ceasefire talks are dominating headlines. Operation Economic Fury is still running. Hengli Petrochemical designated. Shadow fleet sanctioned. Chinese satellite firms hit. Pentagon counts $4.8B in Iranian oil revenue destroyed. Military strikes may have paused. The financial deconstruction has not.

items.title: Oil tops $100 + Aramco CEO issues stark Hormuz warning — markets sell off 2%+ intraday on Iran/CPI then fully recover
items.idea: Oil prices topped $100 again as Iran supply risks intensify. Aramco's CEO issued an unusually direct public statement on the Strait of Hormuz and oil. Today's CPI print (3.8% YoY) stoked an intraday selloff: Nasdaq and Russell 2000 fell over 2% at the lows. By close, both fully recovered — Dow finished green, S&P 500 also positive. J.P. Morgan separately revised gold price targets upward for 2026.
items.angle: The full V-recovery on what should have been a brutal tape (3.8% CPI + $100 oil) is the most important market signal of the session. The Aramco CEO Hormuz comment warrants direct attention — this is not routine press commentary from the world's largest oil producer.
items.tweetA: $100 oil. 3.8% CPI. Nasdaq down 400+ points intraday. Then: full recovery. The tape is telling you something. You can debate the fundamentals, but the market just shrugged off the worst inflation print since 2023 with oil at a century handle. Don't fight that.
items.tweetB: Aramco's CEO is making a public statement on Hormuz while Iran's rial hits 1.87M/dollar and the US blockade costs the IRGC $170M a day. That's not routine press commentary. When the world's largest oil company addresses Hormuz by name — price the optionality.

items.title: Cerebras IPO (20x oversubscribed, pricing today): OpenAI deal is $1B loan + near-free warrants + cloud service — not a hardware sale; 86% revenue concentration
items.idea: Cerebras IPO is 20x oversubscribed, pricing today. The OpenAI deal is structured as a cloud service, not hardware: 750 MW compute capacity over 3 years worth $20B+ base. OpenAI advanced a $1B working capital loan at 6% (waived if repaid through capacity delivery — prepayment dressed as debt). OpenAI received warrants on ~33M shares at $0.00001 strike. Cerebras carries data center infrastructure on its balance sheet. G42 and MBZUAI in the UAE represented 86% of 2025 revenue. WSE-3 specs: 900k cores, 44GB SRAM per wafer, 21 PB/s memory bandwidth. Core operational risk: Cerebras has never run data centers at scale. TSMC foundry lock-in is structural — a decade of custom wafer-level process.
items.angle: The 86% customer concentration + forced cloud model (nobody buying hardware directly) is the fundamental vulnerability. This is a sovereign cloud contract with silicon attached, and the IPO multiple reflects a very specific bull case.
items.tweetA: Cerebras IPO is 20x oversubscribed. But read the deal structure: OpenAI didn't buy hardware. They gave Cerebras a $1B loan and got near-free warrants on 33M shares. Cerebras keeps the data centers on its balance sheet. This is the GroqCloud model. They built it; now they have to operate it.
items.tweetB: 86% of Cerebras' 2025 revenue came from G42 and MBZUAI. That's not a semiconductor company. That's a government-backed cloud contract with custom silicon attached. The question isn't whether the WSE displaces NVIDIA — it's what happens to the multiple if OpenAI doesn't expand past 750 MW.

items.title: Gimlet Labs: neoclouds structurally locked into single silicon by equity terms; d-Matrix Corsair + B200 delivers 4x Pareto frontier shift on GPT-OSS 120B
items.idea: Gimlet Labs (2023, $92M raised, $10M+ ARR) powers OpenAI's Cerebras-based Codex-Spark. Core insight: most neoclouds are locked into single-silicon vendors due to equity terms — hardware amortization is ~70% of annual costs, leaving no room to diversify. Gimlet's two-track model: deploy orchestration software inside frontier labs/hyperscalers/sovereigns AND operate its own mixed-silicon neocloud. On GPT-OSS 120B (8K input, 1K output), running speculative decoder on d-Matrix Corsair while NVIDIA B200s handled the verifier delivered ~4x improvement on the throughput-vs-interactivity Pareto frontier vs GPU-only speculative decode. Sovereign cloud segment (Europe, Middle East, India, Korea) is prime: government funding, emerging local silicon, zero software talent for N-vendor kernel optimization.
items.angle: The equity entanglement of neoclouds is the most underappreciated structural constraint in AI infrastructure. The 4x Pareto shift with d-Matrix is significant — same power envelope, 4x the performance on the right workload shape.
items.tweetA: Most neoclouds can't mix silicon — not technically, but because they gave equity to one vendor and are structurally locked in. Hardware amortization is 70% of their costs; no room to diversify. Gimlet built the neocloud that can. And d-Matrix + B200 just delivered a 4x Pareto frontier shift on a 120B model.
items.tweetB: The inference hardware race isn't NVIDIA vs AMD vs d-Matrix. It's NVIDIA-only stacks vs heterogeneous stacks routing each workload segment to the right chip. Same power envelope. 4x the performance on the right shape. OpenAI already uses this for Codex-Spark. Watch what happens next.

charts.desc: 🔥 CHART WORTH SAVING: Pareto frontier chart showing 3 configs for GPT-OSS 120B — pure GPU disagg, GPU speculative decode, and heterogeneous d-Matrix Corsair + B200. The 4x gap at equivalent interactivity is the whole thesis. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: How to Short a Bubble: Wedge (long rates, not short AI), Victim (Canadian banks at 3x book with PIK-style mortgages), Confirmation (trendline break)
items.idea: Three-part bubble-positioning framework: (A) The Wedge — do not short the bubble; go long what kills it. If AI is the bubble, rates are the wedge — every fat-multiple tech name is a long-duration asset in disguise. (B) The Victim — find the correlated non-convex asset with cheap vol: Canadian banks at 3x price/book with negative amortizing mortgages (borrower pays less than interest, difference capitalizes into principal like a PIK loan) — described as making 2007 American housing look restrained. (C) Confirmation — wait for a trendline break, not a dip. Today's trades: short 5% more SPX + 10% HYG, put spreads on treasuries, sold 5% Canadian banks. Private credit flagged as a 'roach motel' — marks don't move because nobody is forced to mark them.
items.angle: The wedge insight is most actionable: the better trade is not to short AI names but to go long the rate instruments that compress their multiples. Canadian bank thesis with negative amortizing mortgages at 3x book with cheap options is independently interesting.
items.tweetA: The best way to short a bubble is not to short the bubble. Find the Wedge — the thing that kills it — and go long that. If AI is the bubble, rates are the wedge. Every fat-multiple tech name is a long-duration bond in disguise. You don't need to short NVDA. You need to short duration.
items.tweetB: Canadian banks at 3x price/book with negative amortizing mortgages — meaning the borrower pays LESS than the interest, and the gap capitalizes into principal like a PIK loan. The author calls it '2007 America looks restrained.' And the options are still cheap. That's the Victim trade.

charts.desc: Silver trendline break chart referenced. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: The 'Trump TACO trade' is overriding sell-in-May — Hirsch compares AI moment to the 1990s internet productivity boom
items.idea: Jeff Hirsch (Stock Trader's Almanac) argues Trump's pattern — shake things up in Q1, then navigate to keep markets moving through year-end — is currently overriding typical May-October seasonal weakness (the 'Trump TACO trade'). Analogy: 'This is the internet to AI. It's real. There are going to be setbacks, but you can see by the action in tech in the face of Middle East war that the underlying technology has legs.' Best sectors in worst 6 months: utilities (consistent March-October gains), biotech, healthcare, staples. Market breadth widening including small caps, though small-cap outperformance vs large caps may level off.
items.angle: The seasonality override thesis is the most useful contrarian call: if the Trump political cycle genuinely overrides sell-in-May, the consensus defensive repositioning is wrong. Utilities in the worst 6 months is an underrated combination.
items.tweetA: Jeff Hirsch's case: Trump's political cycle is overriding 'sell in May.' He shakes things up Q1, then massages policy to keep markets up through year-end. Small sample, but 'hard to deny.' The consensus is repositioning defensively. Hirsch says that's the wrong playbook.
items.tweetB: We're in the 1990s productivity boom again — except it's AI instead of the internet. That's Jeff Hirsch's frame from the Stock Trader's Almanac. The 90s analogy also implies: the setbacks are real and temporary. And the 2000 endgame is real too. Question is whether we're in '95 or '99.

items.title: 'No Silver Bullets' at 40 years: AI generates 100x code but real productivity gains 'unimpressive' — GTA VI is 12 years; complex software timeline unchanged
items.idea: Brooks' 1986 paper predicted no single technology would deliver a 10x improvement in software productivity, reliability, or simplicity. Revisiting 40 years later: AI generates 100x+ code output, but 'productivity, reliability, and simplicity improvements are a bit unimpressive — at least for now.' GTA VI takes 6-12 years of development despite every modern tool — complex software timelines have not fundamentally shortened, because when capability increases, ambitions scale accordingly. Proposed silent silver bullet: open source + GitHub (2010s). SRE was a silver bullet for Google Search specifically (one outage in 15 years) but not for other Google services. StackOverflow is 'pretty much dead' by 2025.
items.angle: 100x code generation volume vs stagnant real productivity is the key tension. If Brooks' law still holds, AI productivity gains will be incremental compound improvements rather than the step-change the market is pricing. GTA VI is the best single data point: 12 years, all modern tools, still 12 years.
items.tweetA: AI generates 100x the code. Complex software still takes years to ship. GTA VI: 6-12 years in development despite every modern tool. Brooks said in 1986 there are no silver bullets in software — no single development that delivers 10x productivity. 40 years later: is he still right?
items.tweetB: Open source + GitHub may have been the real silver bullet of the 2010s — nobody noticed because it arrived slowly. AI is the loudest proposed silver bullet in history. But the quiet ones (version control, CI/CD, open source) actually moved the needle more than they got credit for. Pattern worth watching.

items.title: All three hyperscalers now native x402 — AWS embeds Coinbase payments in AgentCore; Coinbase projects $4.25B from agentic commerce; Venice +79% on private AI ATH
items.idea: AWS Bedrock AgentCore Payments embedded Coinbase's x402 protocol natively on May 7 — making AWS, Google, and Microsoft all native x402. Settlement on Base in ~200ms, sub-cent per transaction. x402 cumulative metrics: $47.5M processed, 180M transactions, 5K merchants. Coinbase projects $4.25B in revenue from agentic commerce. Venice (VVV) surged +79% in 7 days as private AI demand hit a new ATH on May 11 — AskVenice beat previous ATH by 10%. LayerZero suffered a $292M hack (KelpDAO exploit); $1B migrating to Chainlink CCIP ($LINK). LINK-specific spot ETFs now past $115M combined AUM.
items.angle: When all three hyperscalers adopt a crypto payment protocol natively, the experiment phase ends. The $4.25B Coinbase projection implies agentic commerce alone could be as large as everything Coinbase does today. LINK migration from the LayerZero hack is an immediate narrative catalyst.
items.tweetA: AWS just embedded x402 natively into its managed agent infrastructure. All three hyperscalers are now on x402. Coinbase projects $4.25B in revenue from agentic commerce — more than its current total run rate. 'There will soon be more AI agents transacting than humans.' — Coinbase Head of Infrastructure
items.tweetB: Venice rallied +79% in 7 days on private AI demand hitting a new ATH. People are paying a premium for AI that doesn't surveil them. Separately, the $292M LayerZero hack pushed $1B to Chainlink CCIP. One exploit, one beneficiary. $LINK ETF AUM already past $115M.

charts.desc: x402 cumulative metrics chart and 7-day DeAI market cap performance table. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: TAO now on Solana via Wormhole Sunrise; Grayscale GTAO at 52% premium to NAV ($9.06 vs $5.97); Conviction Locks go live May 13
items.idea: Two TAO catalysts landed May 9: canonical TAO live on Solana via Wormhole Sunrise (Jupiter/Meteora trading, Phantom/Solflare support), and Grayscale reopened GTAO private placements. GTAO currently trades at $9.06 vs NAV of $5.97 = 52% premium, AUM $13.06M. Conviction Locks staking upgrade scheduled May 13 introduces long-duration locked staking for governance weight — designed to compress liquid supply. Bittensor pharma subnet (SN68/NOVA) screened 11 million drug molecules (May 10), triggering +21% 7d.
items.angle: 52% premium to NAV signals extreme scarcity of regulated TAO exposure for accredited investors. Conviction Locks compressing liquid supply + Solana liquidity access + GTAO private placements is a structurally interesting convergence.
items.tweetA: Grayscale's TAO trust is trading at a 52% premium to NAV. Not 5%. Fifty-two. That's how scarce regulated TAO exposure is for accredited investors. TAO just launched on Solana. Conviction Locks go live May 13, compressing liquid supply. Three catalysts in one week.
items.tweetB: A Bittensor subnet screened 11 million drug molecules and the token moved +21% in 7 days. Real-world verifiable output from a decentralized AI network, at pharmaceutical research scale. This is what the verifiable utility thesis in DeAI actually looks like.

items.title: Early stage markets: by the time they're legible enough to map, the best entry points are already closed — look for behavior, not categories
items.idea: a16z speedrun investor Emily Bennett: 'By the time a market is legible enough to be mapped, the most valuable entry points have already closed.' The key signal is behavioral intensity — not apps that people open often, but products that induce structural new ways of working/being/creating/existing. Cited example: OpenClaw, which proved agents could be held to outcomes — spawning agentic workforce pitches within weeks. Three pre-legibility signals: community self-organizes around waitlist without prompting; users write tutorials before official docs exist; small group uses it far more than expected, bending it into unplanned use cases.
items.angle: The framework is most useful as a filter for what is NOW in the behavioral phase before legibility: which categories are post-map (agentic coding, LLM APIs) vs still pre-map. The companies in the weird corners are worth finding before the grid gets published.
items.tweetA: The market map is a trap. By the time a VC publishes a category grid with logos, the best entry points have closed. What you're looking for is behavior that precedes the category: users who can't stop using something nobody has named yet. The founders who win aren't waiting for legibility.
items.tweetB: a16z speedrun's framework: the difference between 'opens often' and 'changes how people exist.' Most apps have the former. Generational companies have the latter. You find it by haunting Discord servers with no business model and subreddits full of workflow complaints nobody has named yet.

items.title: Sutter Health pays $21M for recording patients without consent via ambient AI — Stanford AI Index: healthcare is #1 sector for expected AI positive impact
items.idea: California-based Sutter Health lost a $21M class action lawsuit for using Abridge AI to record patient conversations in exam rooms without proper opt-in consent. Four violations: California Invasion of Privacy Act, Confidentiality of Medical Information Act, Federal Wiretap Act, and Unfair Competition Law. Separate antitrust case for $228M also pending. Stanford's 2026 AI Index: medical care tops the list — among both general public AND AI experts — as the domain where AI will have the most positive impact. The failure: the AI worked exactly as designed; the governance framework to ask whether deployment was permissible was never built.
items.angle: The combination of Stanford AI Index (healthcare = #1 for expected AI positive impact) with the Sutter Health case makes a clean frame: massive public support for AI in healthcare, and an industry destroying that trust tool by tool by buying first and asking later.
items.tweetA: Sutter Health just paid $21M because an AI tool recorded patients without consent. Violated 4 laws. The AI worked perfectly. That's the problem. Stanford's AI Index says healthcare is the #1 sector the public expects AI to benefit most. We're burning that trust tool by tool.
items.tweetB: The Sutter Health case is being read as an AI failure. It's a governance failure. The AI did exactly what it was designed to do. Nobody in the governance chain asked whether they were allowed to deploy it that way. That gap — buying the tool before asking the question — is how you get a $21M judgment.

charts.desc: Stanford AI Index 2026 chart showing AI positive impact expectations by sector. Charts inside — worth opening.

items.title: Plume Nest: RWA-backed institutional yield (Treasuries, private credit, basis strategies) now composable across 4 chains with EtherFi/Perena integrations
items.idea: Plume's Nest protocol converts stablecoin deposits into composable yield-bearing assets backed by tokenized RWAs: US Treasuries, private credit, payment financing, and basis strategies. Recent upgrades expanded supported vaults and extended availability across Ethereum, Solana, BNB Chain, and Plume mainnet. Integrations with EtherFi and Perena mark a structural shift: RWA-backed yield is evolving from standalone product into embedded yield infrastructure — a foundational component of the onchain finance stack.
items.angle: The EtherFi/Perena integrations are the key data point. Yield is becoming middleware, not a product. When institutional RWA yield becomes composable across 4 chains and pluggable into DeFi protocols, it changes the risk/return calculus for onchain capital allocation.
items.tweetA: Plume Nest just expanded RWA-backed yield across Ethereum, Solana, BNB, and Plume — Treasuries, private credit, basis strategies. EtherFi already plugged in. This is what composable finance looks like: institutional yield as middleware, not a wrapper.
items.tweetB: The RWA narrative is maturing from 'tokenized bonds exist' to 'institutional yield is embedded middleware.' Plume Nest integrates into EtherFi and Perena — the yield lives in the protocol stack, not a vault. That's a different paradigm than a T-bill wrapper.
